content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
October 4,
1995
Kayla J. Gillan
Deputy General Counsel
Public Employees Retirement System
Lincoln Plaza,
400
P Street
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
•
Our File No. A-95-304
Dear Ms. Gillan:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Deanna VanValer,
an employee of the Public Employees Retirement
System, under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.-/
QUESTION
Does Ms.
VanValer’s interest in a 401(k) plan sponsored and
administered by her former employer,
The Wyatt Company, create a
conflict of interest which would prevent her from participating in
a decision to award a contract to The Wyatt Company?
CONCLUS ION
No.
Ms. VanValer’s interest in the 401(k) plan does not
create an economic interest in The Wyatt Company.
Therefore,
she
is not prohibited from participating in a decision to award a
contract to
The Wyatt Company.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18995.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CAuF0RNIA95814
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A-95-304
Page2
FACTS
Deanna VanValer ceased employment with The Wyatt Company
(“Wyatt”)
on July 26,
1994,
and began working for the California
Public Employees’ Retirement System
(“PERS”)
as a staff actuary on
August 2,
1994.
While employed with Wyatt,
Ms.
VanValer was entitled to
participate in an employee profit-sharing plan.
The plan is a
qualified plan under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a),
specifically a 401(k)
defined ‘contribution plan.
Wyatt is the
plan administrator.
2
!
A private bank is the plan trustee.
You
informed me during our August 26,
1995, telephone conversation
that the funds are held in the name of the bank trustee on behalf
of the, fund participants.
Wyatt has no access to the funds.
Each
participating individual has an account and is given options as to
how he or she wishes his or her funds to be invested.
Wyatt is
not one of the investment options.
Earnings and losses on the
plan assets are allocated to the plan participants’ individual
accounts.
Ms.
VanValer has approximately $3,000 in her account.
While she worked for Wyatt,
Wyatt made contributions to the
plan but those contributions ceased when she left her employment.
Ms. VanValer has not received any distribution from this plan.
PERS is in the process of developing a request for proposals
‘for a contract for the design of its actuarial software.
Wyatt
has indicated an interest in bidding for this contract.
PERS
would like to utilize Ms. VanValer’s expertise by including her in
the decision making process relating to the award of the contract.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
partiàipating in the making,
or using their official position to
influence a governmental decision in which they know or have
reason to know they have a financial interest.
A public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
2!
Employers are traditionally the administrators of qualified
pension plans.
A plan administrator is responsible for the day—
to-day affairs of the qualified retirement plan.
Duties include
such things as hiring attorneys,
accountants, consultants,
and
actuaries; determining eligibility for plan participation;
advising participants or beneficiaries of,’ their rights or options;
ruling on claims for benefits; keeping service records, benefit
records, vesting records and other participant information.
(Source:
The Pension Answer Book,
9th Edition,
Ch.
16.)


Advice File No.
A—95—304
Page
3
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than ibans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Sections 87103(a)
and (c).)
The term “investment” as used above means,
in pertinent part,
any financial interest in or security issued by a business entity,
including but not limited to common stock, preferred stock,
rights, warrants, options,
debt instruments and any partnership or
other ownership interest owned directly,
indirectly or
beneficially by the public official,
or his or her immediate
family.
The term “investment” does not include a time or demand
deposit in a financial institution, shares in a credit union,
any
insurance policy, interest in a diversified mutual fund registered
with the Securities and Exchange Commission under the Investment
Company Act of 1940 or a common trust fund which is created
pursuant to Section l564of the Financial Code.
(Section 82034.)
The term “income” as used above means,
in pertinent part,
a
payment received,
including but not limited to any salary, wage,
advance,
dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
loan, etc..
The term “income” does not include payments received
under a defined benefit pension plan qualified under Internal
Revenue Code Section 401(a).
(Section 82030.)
The Commission has previously determined that neither an
interest in a private profit sharing plan,
nor the investments
held through it, are “investments” within the meaning of Act, if
the plan is a defined benefit pension plan under Internal Revenue
Code Section 401(a).
(Harris Advice Letter No.
A-82—207,
copy
enclosed.)
This advice is consistent with the Act’s provision
exempting payments under a defined benefit pension plan from the
definition of “income.”
Accordingly, Mrs. VanValer does not have
a potential conflict based solely on her interests in,
or future
payments from, her defined pension plan.


Advice File No.
A—95—304
Page
4
Additionally,
Ms.
VanValer left her employment with Wyatt on
July 26,
1994.
More than
12 months has transpired since
terminating her employment with Wyatt.
Provided she has received
no other payments from Wyatt, the salary she received while
employed with Wyatt will not be the basis for a conflict of
interest.
Absent some other disqualifying economic interest as set
forth in Section 87103,
Ms.
VanValer does not have an economic
interest in The Wyatt Company.
Therefore,
she is not prohibited
from participating in the decisionmaking process relating to the
award of the contract for the design of actuarial software.
I trust this answers your question sufficiently.
If you need
anything further,
or wish to discuss this, please feel free to
contact the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
